title: GPU Compute
rules: keep CPU and GPU responsibilities explicit
define buffer layout before kernel logic
separate upload, execute, and readback stages

primary_when: the request is about compute shaders, kernel dispatch, or GPU-side data processing
the request is about structured buffers or GPU compute workflows
must_not_own_when: the request is mainly about CPU-only scheduling, IMGUI, plugin lifecycle, or assembly parsing

solves: moving bulk parallel work from CPU loops into compute kernels
defining reliable buffer-backed data flow for GPU-side transforms
controlling dispatch, indirect dispatch, readback, and synchronization cost
does_not_solve: shader visual style without compute dataflow

core_principles: Buffer layout is the compute shader ABI.
Kernel contracts should name every input, output, counter, and dirty flag.
Dispatch dimensions should derive from the data count and thread-group size explicitly.
Use indirect dispatch when GPU-produced counts should drive later GPU stages.
Readback is a boundary crossing and must be deliberate.
Compute code should clear or update stage state so rerun scope remains visible.
constraints: Do not bind kernels before required buffers and parameters are valid.
Do not let CPU struct stride and HLSL struct layout diverge.
Do not block on GPU readback in a hot path unless correctness requires it.
Do not leave append counters or dirty flags in stale state between dispatches.
Do not allocate and release large buffers every frame when reuse is possible.
decision_rules: Use gpu-compute when the task centers on kernels, dispatch, buffers, readback, or GPU-side transformation.
Attach gpu-pipeline-architecture when the compute work has multiple stages or feeds indirect rendering.
Use structured buffers for typed records and raw buffers for compact counters or argument inputs.
Use append buffers when a stage compacts a variable visible or valid set.
Use async readback for diagnostics and reserve blocking readback for rare correctness barriers.
Require a clear resize policy whenever input counts can change.

preferred_patterns: single owner responsible for buffer allocation and release
ensure-buffer methods keyed by count and stride
append counter reset before compacting stages
GPU counters plus throttled async readback for diagnostics
avoid_patterns: ad hoc GraphicsBuffer creation inside unrelated feature code
magic stride values not tied to struct layout
CPU readback after every dispatch to drive the next dispatch
stale dirty flags causing stages to silently skip or rerun

recipes.name: Build a compute stage contract
recipes.when: Use before implementing a new compute kernel or dispatch path.
steps: Define the stage's input buffers, output buffers, and scalar parameters.
Define count, stride, and target type for every buffer.
Define dispatch group size and how group count is computed.
Define which dirty flag or state enables the stage.
Define which counters or outputs downstream stages consume.
Define validation and failure messages for missing kernels or buffers.

recipes.name: Add an append-buffer compaction stage
recipes.when: Use when a kernel must produce a variable-length set such as visible or valid indices.
steps: Allocate an append buffer sized to the maximum candidate count.
Reset the append counter before dispatch.
Append only compact indices or small records from valid threads.
Copy the append count to a raw count buffer when downstream args need it.
Avoid CPU readback unless diagnostics specifically request it.

recipes.name: Use indirect dispatch from GPU-produced count
recipes.when: Use when one GPU stage determines the workload size for a later GPU stage.
steps: Copy or write the source count into a count buffer.
Run a small args kernel that converts count to thread groups.
Store group counts in an indirect arguments buffer.
Dispatch the downstream kernel with DispatchIndirect.
Validate zero-count and minimum-one-dispatch behavior deliberately.

pitfalls: Using CPU readback as routine stage glue.
Changing C# struct layout without updating HLSL struct layout.
Dispatching with stale group counts after input size changes.
Treating diagnostic counters as required pipeline state.
Ignoring buffer release on teardown or domain reload.
review_checklist: Are all buffers named with role, stride, count, and target type?
Are append counters and dirty flags reset or cleared correctly?
Does the pipeline avoid hot-path blocking readback?
Is buffer ownership and release unambiguous?

references.title: GPU Buffer Contracts
references.interpretation: Use this first for buffer layout, count, target, and ownership decisions.
references.why: Compute correctness depends on explicit buffer ABI.

references.title: GPU Pipeline Architecture
references.interpretation: Use when compute stages are part of a broader staged GPU pipeline.
references.why: It connects individual kernels to stage ordering and rerun policy.

references.title: GPU Diagnostics
references.interpretation: Use when adding counters, readback, or failure reporting.
references.why: It keeps debugging from introducing synchronization stalls.
